coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient spin field-effect transistor based on nanographene and hBN heterostructures: spintronic and quantum transport properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Physical Society of the Republic of China (Taiwan). Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Physical Society of the Republic of China (Taiwan). Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, Hazem
Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Teleb, Nahed H.
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Zhilong, Wang
Liu, Yushen
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: Intense efforts are being undertaken to design atomic-scale nanodevices in response to the demand for novel electronic/spintronic devices to address the problems facing the current devices. In this work, we construct new spintronic nanodevices based on 2D heterostructures from graphene and hBN quantum dots. The heterostructures are ferromagnetic spin-ordered semiconductors with nonzero spin originating from the unpaired edge electrons of the graphene quantum dots. The net spin and its distribution on different layers of the heterostructures are controllable. For instance, doping the hBN layer in the lateral graphene-hBN-graphene structure with C-atoms can boost the net spin and facilitate its distribution on the three layers. The electronic properties are also affected where half metallicity has been achieved in this system. The I-V characteristics of the lateral G-hBN heterostructure show an oscillating spin-up current that is significantly higher than the regular spin-down current. Doping G-hBN with C-atoms significantly enhances the current magnitude and removes the oscillation due to the decreased net spin by the dopants. We demonstrate a field effect transistor from the lateral G-hBN-G heterostructure that operates based on quantum tunneling from the G-layer through the hBN-layer. The device works as a spin filter at zero gate voltage. A significant enhancement of the drain current is achieved by applying a positive gate voltage. This device shows an ON/OFF ratio of up to 1010 making it an ideal applicant for future spintronic nanodevices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D heterostructures
Graphene and hBN quantum dots
DFT
Quantum transport
Spin filter
Spin field effect transistor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361, 62174016), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. H. Abdelsalam thanks Xiaowen Shi (from HZWTECH) for his help and discussions regarding this study.

item-info.ce:copyright: The Physical Society of the Republic of China (Taiwan)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Vertical and lateral heterostructures based on triangular graphene and hBN quantum dots before and after doping with odd (C, and 3C) and even numbers of C-atoms.
Spin density difference distribution of vertical (a-h) and lateral heterostructures (i-p). The values of the net spin are indicated for each structure.
The electronic density of states and the corresponding energy gaps of the vertical bilayer (a-d) and the trilayer (e-h) heterostructures from T-G and T-hBN.
The HOMO and LUMO of the spin-up molecular orbitals for vertical bilayer (a-d) and trilayer (e-h).
The electronic density of states of the bilayer (a-d) and trilayer (e-h) heterostructures from laterally connected T-G and T-hBN layers doped with even and odd numbers of C-atoms.
The spin-up HOMO and LUMO distributions of lateral heterostructures constructed from two (a-d) and three T-G and T-hBN layers (e-i).

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of vertical and lateral Heterostructures utilizing triangular graphene and hBN quantum dots, pre and post-doping with odd (C and 3C) and even (4C) numbers of carbon atoms.
Schematic diagram of the Electrode-T-G-hBN-Electrode device.
(a) I-V curves for spin up and spin down currents through the Electrode-T-G-hBN-Electrode device subjected to forward bias. (b-i) spin up and spin down transmission curves at various bias voltages.
The same as in Fig. 9 but for Electrode-T-G-hBN-4C-Electrode device.

Filed effect transistor using the T-2G-hBN lateral heterostructure with left/right source/drain and top/bottom gate. (a) schematic representation of the device. (b, c) Id-Vg curves for the spin-up and spin-down currents. (d, e) Id-Vg characteristics after doping with 4C, namely T-2G-hBN-4C. (f-j) the Id-Vd curves at different values of Vg before and after doping.

Some important structural parameters like bond length (Å), dihedral, and bond angles (degrees) for vertical and lateral Heterostructures utilizing triangular G and hBN Quantum Dots, pre and post-doping with odd (C and 3C) and even (4C) numbers of carbon atoms.

The calculated excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for vertical and lateral heterostructures, pre and post-doping with odd (C and 3C) and even (4C) numbers of carbon atoms. A and B refer to the spin-up and spin-down electrons.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient spin field-effect transistor based on nanographene and hBN heterostructures: spintronic and quantum transport properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intense efforts are being undertaken to design atomic-scale nanodevices in response to the demand for novel electronic/spintronic devices to address the problems facing the current devices. In this work, we construct new spintronic nanodevices based on 2D heterostructures from graphene and hBN quantum dots. The heterostructures are ferromagnetic spin-ordered semiconductors with nonzero spin originating from the unpaired edge electrons of the graphene quantum dots. The net spin and its distribution on different layers of the heterostructures are controllable. For instance, doping the hBN layer in the lateral graphene-hBN-graphene structure with C-atoms can boost the net spin and facilitate its distribution on the three layers. The electronic properties are also affected where half metallicity has been achieved in this system. The I-V characteristics of the lateral G-hBN heterostructure show an oscillating spin-up current that is significantly higher than the regular spin-down current. Doping G-hBN with C-atoms significantly enhances the current magnitude and removes the oscillation due to the decreased net spin by the dopants. We demonstrate a field effect transistor from the lateral G-hBN-G heterostructure that operates based on quantum tunneling from the G-layer through the hBN-layer. The device works as a spin filter at zero gate voltage. A significant enhancement of the drain current is achieved by applying a positive gate voltage. This device shows an ON/OFF ratio of up to 1010 making it an ideal applicant for future spintronic nanodevices.

ce:section.ce:para: Materials with designed properties are a challenging task however it is being extensively investigated because such designed materials have the potential to revolutionize the performance of nanodevices [1,2]. The ultrathin and ultralight two-dimensional materials with their unique properties [3-5] are promising platforms for constructing heterostructures with tailored properties for different applications [6-8]. The family of 2D materials now contains hundreds of materials with a wide spectrum of properties that can be combined to form various heterostructures with new properties and applications beyond their constituent layers. 2D heterostructures can be divided into two groups, vertical heterostructures where 2D layers are stacked above each other and are upheld by Van der Waals forces. In this way, arbitrary 2D layers can be stacked together using different techniques including physical vapor deposition and exfoliation-transfer technique [9,10]. On the other hand, the second group, lateral heterostructures are more challenging to synthesize as atoms from different layers should bond together to form well-stitched lateral heterostructures [11,12]. 2D heterostructures are potential candidates for future electronic and optoelectronic devices due to their exceptional electronic and optical properties [13-17]. For instance, graphene transistors with ultrahigh mobility are achieved by encapsulating graphene with hexagonal boron nitride [18-20]. Moreover, photovoltaics and light-emitting diodes can be constructed from 2D heterostructures made up of optically active semiconducting layers with different work functions and band alignment [21-24].

Field effect transistors based on graphene-boron nitride heterostructures exhibit many interesting aspects such as boosting the on/off current ratio. Britnell et al., have synthesized field-effect transistors in which a thin tunneling barrier from hexagonal boron nitride is sandwiched between graphene layers [20]. This device exhibits room temperature switching ratios of ∼50% and ∼ 10000, respectively. Ma et al. reported the realization of on/off current rations over 104 in MoS2 vertical transistors. The authors showed that the off-state tunneling current can be suppressed by designing the barrier height via a Van der Waals metal contact. As a result of the difference between the work function of the metal (Pt) and the MoS2 layer, the Schottky barrier becomes high enough to suppress the direct tunneling currents [25].

2D heterostructures constitute ideal platforms not only for engineering electronic properties but also for spintronic ones [26-28]. Graphene is an exceptional spin channel because of its large spin diffusion length, gate-tunable carrier concentration, and high carrier mobility [29,30]. 2D transition metal dichalcogenides (e.g. MoS2 and WS2) with semiconducting and gap have strong spin-orbit coupling (SOC) which facilitates the electrical generation and manipulation of spins [31]. By comping graphene and WS2 in one 2D heterostructure, it was observed that graphene acquires spin-orbit coupling up to 17 mV which is three orders of magnitude higher than its intrinsic value [32,33]. In such Van der Waals heterostructure, the extraordinarily high mobility of graphene is kept unchanged and at the same time, the SOC is significantly enhanced allowing for the control of the spin current by the electric field. This proximity spin-orbit coupling results in a spin hall effect even at room temperature, therefore opening the door toward spin field effect transistors.

In this work, we investigate the spintronic properties of lateral and vertical 2D heterostructures from triangular graphene and hBN quantum dots. It is known that triangular graphene quantum dots with zigzag terminations have intrinsic spin due to the inequality between the number of atoms in sublattice A (NA) and the number in sublattice B (NB) [33-38]. This intrinsic spin in addition to the controllable energy gap by quantum size effect makes these quantum dots promising for building various spintronic devices such as spin filters and spin field effect transistors. We also consider doping of the hBN layer with C-atoms which not only control the electronic and spintronic properties of the heterostructure but also the tunneling barrier height in the constructed graphene-hBN-graphene field effect transistor.

The structural, magnetic, electronic, and optical properties of the vertical and lateral heterojunctions are investigated using DFT calculations as implemented in Gaussian 16 [39]. The Becke-Three-Parameters-Lee-Yang-Parr hybrid functional (B3LYP) [40,41] and the basis set of 6-31g [42,43] are employed for the calculation because of the good representation of the electronic structure of C-based materials and moderate computational power required [44-46]. To account for the long-range Van der Waals interactions, Grimme's dispersion correction (gd3) is added to the B3LYP functional [47]. The UV-vis absorption spectra are obtained by performing time-dependent DFT calculations for the first 20 excited states. The quantum transport properties of the lead-heterostructure-lead device are investigated based on density functional theory and nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) calculations using Nanodcal software [48,49]. The local density approximation (LDA) is used to describe the exchange-correlation function and the cut of energy is set to 80 Hartree. In the self-consistent calculation, the K points for the electrode were set to (1,1,100) points and (1,1,3) points for the Brillouin zone.

Vertical and lateral heterostructures constructed from triangular graphene and hBN are shown in Fig. 1 . The numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4 represent the sites on which substitutional doping of B atoms with 1, 3, and 4 C-atoms will be made. For triangular graphene/hBN vertical heterostructure, we use the acronym T-G-hBN-V. For lateral heterostructure, the acronym is T-G-hBN, where T represents triangular so T-G and T-hBN will also be used to refer to triangular graphene and triangular hBN, respectively. Increasing the number of layers is expressed by adding that number in front of the corresponding layer and doping by adding C (carbon), such as T-2G-hBN-C-V shown in Fig. 1 (c).

First, we start by analyzing the structural parameters, see Table 1 , of the optimized structures shown in Fig. 1. Regarding the vertical heterostructure, it is noted that the optimized structures prefer the AB-stacking type even with an initial AA alignment of layers. Within each layer, bond angles such as C15-C16-C17, C18-C19-C20, B5-N6-B7, and B7-N11-B12 consistently approximated 120.00°, signifying intralayer sp2 hybridization. Furthermore, a distinct trend emerged in the bond lengths. The B5-N6 bond length was observed to exceed the C15-C16 bond length, a result of the inherent variations in atomic sizes and bonding characteristics between boron and carbon. Notably, the inherent planarity of G and hBN units themselves (Fig. 1a and b) was observed, whereas all other vertical and lateral heterostructures displayed non-planar conformations. The dihedral angles C17-C18-C19-C20 and B12-N11-B7-N6, serving as critical planarity indicators, consistently fell within a narrow range of 177.096° to 179.929° for G and hBN across all studied structures. In lateral heterostructures, relatively minor rotations of each G unit out of the hBN unit were observed (Fig. 1 (e-h)), with dihedral angles of N6-B5-C15-C16 ranging from 26.433 to 33.312 degrees. These rotations were identified as mechanisms to mitigate steric hindrance.

The investigation into the stability of both vertical and lateral heterostructures involving T-G-hBN and its derivatives centered around the computation of their binding energies (BE) and the vibrational frequencies. The BE values were determined through the equation: BE = (NCEC + NBEB + NNEN + NHEH - Et)/Nt, where NC, NB, NN, NH, and Nt denote the quantities of carbon, boron, nitrogen, hydrogen, and the total number of atoms, respectively. EC, EB, EN, EH, and Et represent the total energies of carbon, boron, nitrogen, hydrogen atoms, and the final compound, respectively. The calculated BE values are consolidated in Table 1. Our analysis of these computed binding energies was conducted to validate the stability and characteristics of both T-G-hBN and its derivatives. The positive binding energies, spanning from 6.815 to 7.128 eV, signify the formation of T-G-hBN and its associated compounds in a stable configuration. The dynamical stability is also confirmed by performing frequency calculations from which we obtain the infrared (IR) spectra, see Fig. S1. The vibrational frequencies responsible for the IR-peaks are an efficient tool to test the geometrical stability of a certain structure, where if they have real positive values then their square are positive and the optimized structure represents a minimum on the potential energy surface (PES). If they have some imaginary frequencies (negative values here), then their square is negative implying a negative local curvature on the PES that leads to a decrease of the energy as the atoms are displaced [50]. As can be seen in Fig. S1 the vibrational modes are real for all the considered structures which means that they represent a local minimum on the structural PES and the structures are dynamically stable.

The magnetic properties of these systems are investigated by calculating and comparing the ground state energy of different spin states, the one with the lowest ground state energy is the magnetic state of the system. Namely the calculations are performed for closed-shell (S=0) representing the nonmagnetic state, open shell (S=0) for antiferromagnetic ordering, and for a non-zero spin with different multiplicities (e.g. triplet, quintet, etc.). Starting with the vertical heterostructures, the bilayer T-G-hBN-V has ferromagnetic spin ordering with net spin S=2.

The ferromagnetic properties originate from C-atoms at the zigzag edges that have frustrated electrons unable to form pi-bonds with neighbor atoms [34,35]. The number of these unpaired electrons depends on the size of the triangular graphene is consistent with Lieb's theorem [51], in the current structure there are four electrons so S=2. The spin density difference shown in Fig. 2 (a) confirms that the net spin in this bilayer is totally from the triangular graphene layer. Doping with one C-atom decreases the net spin to S=1.5 which is simply due to the pairing of the unpaired electron from the doped C-atom with one of the triangular graphene (T-G) unpaired electrons. It was expected that the net spin would increase by these additional C-atoms, especially if it is isolated in the hBN layer, however, the interaction between different spins shows a decrease in the net spin. In this case, the structure with sextet spin state (S=2.5) is the first excited spin state with ground state energy lower negative than that of the quartet state (S=1.5) by -0.97 eV. Increasing the doped C-atoms to three increases the net spin to S=2.5 where the additional spin comes from only one of the doped C-atoms while the other two atoms passivate each other. The contribution to the spin density from the hBN layer starts to appear after doping, In Fig. 2 (c) small blue cubes representing the spin-up density appear on the doped C-atoms and neighbor N atoms. This contribution significantly increases by doping with 3C atoms as presented in Fig. 2 (c). This means that the hBN layer becomes ferromagnetic after doping with an odd number of C-atoms. Doping with an even number (4C) does not affect the net spin where the unpaired electrons from the 4C atoms interact together and form an antiferromagnetic spin-ordered layer from doped hBN. This antiferromagnetic layer now contains spin density from both spin-up (blue) and spin-down (red) orbitals as seen in Fig. 2 (d). Heterostructure from three layers with one hBN sandwiched between two T-G has a net spin S=4 with spin density distribution only on the T-G layers as seen in Fig. 2 (e). doping hBN with one C-atom decreases the net spin to 3.5 similar to the case of bilayer. However, in contrast to bilayer, doping with 3C-atoms significantly decreases the net spin to 2.5 which could be a result of the stronger interactions between the doped hBN layer and the upper and lower T-G layers. The spin distributions shown in Fig. 2 (f, g) indicate that the doped hBN layer in the trilayer system has ferromagnetic spin ordering dominated by the spin-down electrons. The 4C doping in the trilayer is similar to that in the bilayer, where the 4C prefers to interact with themselves in the same layer instead of interacting with vertical layers leading to no change in the net spin originating from the two T-G layers.

The lateral heterostructures show a systematic behavior under the effect of doping. Similar to the vertical bilayer, T-G-hBN has a net spin S= 2 that decreases/increases to S=1.5/2.5 after doping hBN with one/three C-atom or kept unchanged in 4C doped hBN. The corresponding density distributions are shown in Fig. 2 (i-l). On the other hand, the spin ordering in the lateral trilayers is different from that in vertical where the net spin in the undoped T-2G-hBN decreases/increases from S=4 to S= 3.5/5 after doping with one/three C-atom or not changed in 4C doped hBN similar to bilayers. It is worth noting that the single layer of hBN doped with four C atoms is nonmagnetic, however after incorporation in heterostructures containing layers with ferromagnetic spin it becomes magnetic with significant spin distribution on it, see for example Fig. 4 (h, p). Based on the spin distributions we can say that the current heterostructures represent: (a) ferromagnetically coupled nonmagnetic-ferromagnetic layers (e.g. Fig. 2 a, e), (b) ferromagnetically coupled ferromagnetic- ferromagnetic layers (see Fig. 2 c, k), and induced ferromagnetic- ferromagnetic coupling as in Fig. 4 (h, p). These effects which arise due to interactions among layers are expected to show interesting properties in the spin transport properties that will be discussed later.

Now we investigate the electronic properties based on the identified magnetic state. The electronic density of states (DOS) and the spin up/spin down energy gaps (Egα/Egβ) are shown in Fig. 3 for the vertical heterostructures. Substitutional doping with even and odd numbers of C-atoms provides interesting and significant effects on the DOS and the energy gaps. The wide and almost similar spin-up/down energy gaps in the unmodified T-G-hBN-V (Fig. 3 a) experience considerable unequal alternations by doping. For instance, doping with one C-atom decreases the spin-up energy gap from 3.37 eV to 2.08 eV while the spin-down is almost closed, Egβ=0.71 eV. The decrease of spin-down energy gap increases by increasing the number of doped C-atoms reaching Egβ=0.61 eV in T-G-hBN-4C-V, see Fig. 4 d. This effect indicates that half metallicity can be achieved in these heterostructures by doping with C-atoms.

The density of states is also affected by doping where the low energy density peaks that were contributed only by the T-G layer experience noticeable changes in both intensity and origin. By comparing the DOS in Fig. 3 (a, b), we can say that the two read peaks (from T-G layers) representing the HOMO and LUMO in the spin-up DOS become one red for HOMO and one blue (from T-hBN layer) for LUMO. This behavior of the low energy density peaks is also observed in the HOMO and LUMO distributions shown in Fig. 4 (a, b). Where both the HOMO and LUMO of the spin-up orbitals distribute on the T-G layers before doping while after doping the HOMO distributes on one T-G layer and the LUMO distributes on the T-hBN layer. The spin-down HOMO and LUMO distribution on T-G layers before and after doping agree also with the results from DOS, the related plots are provided in Fig. S2 in the supplementary. Increasing the number of doped C-atoms increases the contribution of T-hBN to the low energy peaks of the DOS as can be seen in Fig. 3 (c, d). Of course, the new blue density peak from T-hBN is mainly from the interactive electronic states of the doped C-atoms. In this case, the contribution to the HOMO/LUMO from T-hBN is from C-atoms and neighbor N-atoms as seen in Fig. 4 (c, d).

The density of states of the trilayer heterostructures (Fig. 4 e) show also wide semiconductor spin up/down energy gaps (3.35/3.31) that decrease unequally by doping similarly to bilayer but with even lower spin down energy. The lowest spin-down energy gap observed in this trilayer system is Egβ=0.43 eV after doping with one C atom (Fig. 3 f). The other two cases have a comparable Egβ of 0.52 and 0.57 eV as seen in Fig. 3 (g, h). The spin-up HOMO/LUMO distributions in Fig. 4 (e-h) show that the T-hBN layer starts to contribute to only the LUMO after doping with one C atom, increasing the dopant to 4 C atoms making T-hBN participle to both the HOMO and LUMO. Interestingly in the unmodified vertical heterostructures, the spin down low energy molecular orbitals are contributed by both T-G and T-hBN which can be seen from the DOS in Fig. 3 (a, e) and the HOMO/LUMO distributions in Fig. S2 (a, e). This behavior is different from the conclusion obtained from our previous work on lateral T-G/T-hBN heterostructure which states that the T-hBN layer with its large insulating energy gap acts as confining potential for T-G molecular orbitals [52]. This result is also found for the lateral heterostructures considered in this work. The main reason for this difference between the two cases could be the higher Van der Waals interactions in the vertical heterostructures that decrease the energy difference between the antiferromagnetically ordered electrons from both T-G and T-hBN layers.

The DOS and the energy gaps of the lateral heterostructures are shown in Fig. 5 before and after doping with C atoms. Before, discussing their electronic properties, it is worth noting that the energy gap of the unmodified T-G-hBN structure calculated here using the B3LYB is lower than the one obtained in our previous work using M062X [52]. For instance, the spin energy gap of T-G-hBN equals 3.31 eV here while in ref [52] it is equal to 4.87 eV. Additionally, the ground state energy of the heterostructure using the B3LYB is lower than that of M062X, therefore the former is considered more accurate for the current systems. Similar to the spin-up DOS in vertical bilayer heterostructures, the contribution to the low energy peaks start to appear as the LUMO in T-G-hBN-C, HOMO, and LUMO in T-G-hBN-3C, LUMO in T-G-hBN-4C, see the evolution of the low energy blue peak in Fig. 5 (a-d).

The corresponding HOMO and LUMO confirming this behavior is shown in Fig. 5 (a-d). However, spin-down energy gaps in the lateral bilayers experience a higher decrease than the vertical ones, reaching 0.27 eV in T-G-hBN-4C.

The case of lateral trilayers shown in Fig. 5 (e-h) records an even lower decrease of the spin-down energy gap of 0.14 eV by doping rendering lateral heterostructure promising for semimetalic applications. Moreover, the spin-down HOMO and LUMO distribution on both layers (see Fig. S3) gives another privilege for the possibility of improving the spin-down transport properties by doping.

In this section, extensive investigations were conducted into the UV-Vis absorption spectra of diverse vertical and lateral heterostructures, all based on triangular (T) graphene (G) and hBN quantum dots. The study encompassed configurations both before and after doping with varying quantities of carbon atoms. The examined structures include T-G-hBN-V, T-G-hBN-C-V, T-G-hBN-3C-V, T-G-hBN-4C-V, T-2G-hBN-V, T-2G-hBN-C-V, T-2G-hBN-3C-V, T-2G-hBN-4C-V, T-G-hBN, T-G-hBN-C, T-G-hBN-3C, T-G-hBN-4C, T-2G-hBN, T-2G-hBN-C, T-2G-hBN-3C, and T-2G-hBN-4C. The resulting spectra are graphically presented in Fig. 2, while the absorption parameters are summarized in Table 2 . Our primary objective was to scrutinize the impact of augmenting the count of doping carbon atoms on the UV-Vis absorption spectra within both vertical and lateral T-G-hBN heterostructures. Additionally, we aimed to explore the influence of G and hBN stacking arrangements in both the vertical and lateral orientations. The absorption spectra offer direct insights into the electronic characteristics of the materials and stem from electronic transitions between occupied and unoccupied molecular orbitals (MOs). Consequently, the variations observed in the optical absorption peaks are anticipated to correlate with electronic alterations resulting from changes in the number of doping carbon atoms and the stacking geometry. Our investigation unveiled a significant influence of the doping carbon atom count on the absorption spectra of vertical and lateral T-G-hBN heterostructures. This influence manifested as a redshift, as evident in Fig. 2 and Table 2. Notably, T-G-hBN-C-V, T-G-hBN-3C-V, and T-G-hBN-4C-V exhibited redshifts in their absorption spectra when compared to the absorption spectrum of T-G-hBN-V. The specific λmax values corresponding to these transitions were measured at 511.03 nm, 526.45 nm, and 632.24 nm, correlating to H-2→L (A), H→L+2 (A), and H→L (A) transitions. In contrast, the absorption spectrum of T-G-hBN-V displayed a λmax at 453.37 nm, associated with the H→L+2 (A) transition. In a comparison between the absorption spectra of vertically and laterally stacked T-G-hBN configurations, the results presented in Fig. 2 and Table 2 demonstrated a consistent redshift in the absorption spectrum of vertical stacking compared to lateral stacking. This trend held except for T-G-hBN-C-V and T-2G-hBN-V, where an opposite behavior was observed.

The current-voltage characteristics are investigated here to show the effect of doping on the spin-up and spin-down currents through T-G-hBN before and after doing with C-atoms. The schematic representation of the device is shown in Fig. 8, where the two electrodes and the scattering regions are in the same planes and connected by C-C sigma bonds.

The current passing through the system in the non-equilibrium state is determined by summing the transmission probabilities for electron states from one electrode to the other within the energy window
μ L
−
μ R
=
|
e V
|
, where
μ L
and
μ R
are the electrochemical potentials of the left and right electrodes, respectively, under the applied voltage V. Thus the spin-dependent current is given by:
(1)
I σ
=
e
2
h
T σ
(
μ 1
−
μ 2
)
Where
T σ
=
∑ i
T
i σ
is the total electron transmittance probability with spin state σ ,
T
i σ
represents the electron transmittance probability of the ith intrinsic channel. We used armchair graphene nanoribbons doped with N-atoms as electrodes where the doped N-atoms increase the π-electrons that convert the semiconductor armchair leads to conductors. The schematic representation of the device is shown in Fig. 8.

The I-V curves of the Electrode-T-G-hBN-Electrode have been calculated at different values of applied forward bias which can be seen in Fig. 9 (a). Let's start the discussion by voltage range from 0-0.6 V. It is observed that spin-up electrons have higher conductance than that of the spin-down ones with three orders of magnitude currents higher than. For instance, the maximum value of the spin-up current calculated at a bias voltage of 0.6 V is ∼ 48 nA while that for spin-down is ∼25 pA. This significant difference between spin-up and spin-down conductance is mainly a result of the unpaired edge electrons from the T-G which are added to the spin-up orbitals. Further increase of the applied voltage shows a steady increase in the spin-down current with a maximum value of 1.1 (nA). On the other hand, the spin-up current experiences a noticeable oscillation by increasing the bias. This oscillation is expected to be a result of the oscillation of the energy gap between the edge states similar to our previous results obtained for zigzag-nanoribbons [53]. Moreover, the transmission curves shown in Fig. 9 (b) at zero bias indicate that the spin-up transmission peaks around the Fermi level (0 eV) are much stronger than the spin-down ones. Applying a bias voltage strongly increases the intensity of the spin-up peak at ∼ -3 eV while the neighbor spin-down transmission peak is almost unaffected, see Fig. 9 (c). The effect of bias on the spin-down low energy transmission peak starts to appear at 0.6 V (Fig. 9 d) where both the spin-up and spin-down peaks at ∼ -3 V are almost the same.

Since the applied voltage on the device separates the left and right electrodes' chemical potentials, a bias window close to the Fermi level is created. The magnitude of the bias current is given by the bias window's integral area. It is observed that no transmission peaks line inside the bias window up to a bias voltage of 0.6 V. Then the observed current is a tunneling current through the wide band gap hBN layer. Further increase of the bias voltage to 1 V, see Fig. 9 e, introduces a small spin-up transmission peak into the bias window. Interestingly, this peak disappears after increasing the bias to 1.5 V which is similar to the oscillating behavior of the current. At 2 V a small portion of the spin up and spin down peaks inter the bias windows and gradually increases by increasing the bias up to 3 V as seen in Fig. 9 (g-i). It is also noted that by increasing the bias voltage, the transmission spectrum shifts to negative electron energy. To provide further confirmation that the origin of the oscillating current is the unpaired spin electrons, we plot in Fig. S4 (a) the I-V curves under applied reverse bias. We can see that the oscillation in the current disappears in the range of the applied voltage and the magnitude of the currents is very small compared to the values in the forward bias case. This is mainly a result of the conducting electrons in this case which come from the insulating hBN layer. The behavior of the transmission spectrum under the effect of the reverse bias is also regular where the transmission peaks start to appear in the bias window at 1V and then gradually these peaks increase by increasing the voltage as seen in Fig. S4 (b-i).

After doping the hBN layer with few C-atoms, the oscillation of the spin-up current disappears, and the magnitude of both the spin-up and down current significantly increases as seen in Fig. 10 (a). This is mainly a result of the decrease of the hBN energy gap by doping. This decrease of the energy gap is followed by a decrease of the Schottky barrier height leading eventually to the increase of the current. The Schottky barrier height between the interface of graphene and the hBN layers is defined as the difference between the graphene nanoflakes' work function and the hBN electron affinity. It is also equal to the minimum of the conduction band energy of hBN minus the Fermi level. Based on this definition the spin up/down Schottky barrier for T-G-hBN equals 3.49/3.07 eV. This high value of the barrier heights is mainly a result of the wide insulating band gap of the pristine hBN nanoflake. After doping with 4-C atoms, the barrier height decreases to 0.89/0.18 eV which increases the current in T-G-hBN-4C as seen in Fig. 10 (a). The transmission spectrum at 0V shows spin-up and spin-down peaks with almost the same intensity. A small portion of the spin-up peaks start to appear in the bias window at 0.4 V, and then the contribution increases by the applied voltage. Therefore, doping is an efficient tool to control the potential barrier by the insulating hBN layer and facilitate quantum transport through the constructed device.

The spin field-effect transistor (SFETs) is one of the most important devices based on the constructed heterostructures that will be covered here. The schematic structure of the SFETs is shown in Fig. 11 (a) where the gate voltage is applied on the top/bottom and the source/drain contacts are connected to the left/right of the lateral T-2G-hBN heterostructure. The effect of doping the hBN layer with 4C atoms on the I-V curves is also considered. The working principle of the current FETs relies on quantum tunneling [20,54] from the spin-polarized GQDs through the insulating hBN-QDs. A bias voltage Vd applied to T-G layers through the source and drain contacts (zigzag graphene nanoribbons) gives rise to a tunnel current through the hBN layer. Fig. 11 (b, c) shows the drain spin-up and spin-down currents (Id) as a function of Vd at different values of Vg. Starting with Vg=0, the current has very small values reaching its maximum of ∼ 2 × 10−2 pA at Vd=0.2 V for spin up and 1.3 × 10−4 pA for spin down current. The considerable difference between the spin-up and spin-down currents is a result of the unpaired electrons from the T-G that are added to the spin-up electrons. Therefore, spin filter devices can be constructed from T-G/ T-hBN heterostructure where the spin-up current is allowed in T-G-hBN and T-2G-hBN while the spin-down current is almost blocked, see Fig. 9 (a) and Fig. 11 (b,c).

Increasing Vg leads, in general, to an increase of the Id currents reaching 27.8 (pA) at Vg=20 V, see Fig. 11 (b). It is noted from Fig. 11 (c) that the spin-down current significantly increases by applying Vg. An electric field applied in the y-direction would have a similar effect on the electronic properties as the applied Vg here. In our previous work, we show that the y-electric field shifts the low-energy molecular orbitals of the hBN layer toward the Fermi level and thus provokes their contribution to electronic transport [52]. Doping with 4C atoms changes that case, where spin up current at Vg=0 V is significantly lower than spin down current with eight orders of magnitude lower values even at Vd equals to 0.2 V as shown in Fig. 11 (d, e). Consequently, T-2G-hBN-4C is also a promising spin filter allowing only spin down current. After doping, the spin-up HOMO is localized on the right T-G layer (Fig. 6 i), then transport from the left T-G layer to the right T-G is then very low. On the other hand, the spin-down HOMO distributes in the three layers (Fig. S3 i) which facilitates the transport from the left to right electrode. Moreover, the applied Vg substantially enhances the Id current, especially for the spin-up current.

The subplots in Fig. 11 (f-i) represent the drain current (Id) as a function of gate voltage (Vg) at a fixed bias voltage Vd=0.1 V for T-2G-hBN before and after doping. A positive (negative) gate voltage applied to the hBN layer leads to shifting of the molecular orbitals toward (away from) Fermi level and carrier concentrations increase (decrease) there. Then the tunnel barrier by the T-G-hBN will decrease/increase and the tunnel current (Id) increases/decreases as seen in Fig. 11 (f-i) for both spin-up and spin-down electrons. This behavior is accompanied by an oscillation in the current which is mainly a result of the unpaired edge electrons. One of the main parameters that define the efficiency of a transistor is its ON/OFF ratio. According to the obtained results here and the very low current at applied negative Vg, an exceptional ON/OFF ∼1010 has been achieved in the pristine and doped T-G-hBN lateral heterostructure making them ideal applicants for high-frequency electronics. It is worth noting that we considered also the no spin case for changes in the current as a function of Vd and Vg as seen in Fig. S5 (a) and (b), respectively. The magnitude of the current is nearly the same as in the spin-polarized case (Fig. 7
).

In this work, novel heterostructures are constructed from triangular graphene quantum dots, that hold ferromagnetic spin ordering, and the nonmagnetic-insulator hBN quantum dots. The electronic, spintronic, and quantum transport properties are investigated using density functional theory and nonequilibrium Green's function calculations. The stability of the constructed vertical and lateral heterostructures is confirmed by the positive binding energy and the real vibrational frequencies obtained from the infrared spectra. The constructed systems show many interesting and controllable electronic, optical, and spintronic properties. Such as half metallicity and the controlled spin distribution observed in lateral trilayer built from graphene-hBN-graphene (G-hBN-G) after doping hBN with few C-atoms. Quantum transport through the lateral G-hBN heterostructure indicates an oscillating spin-up current, due to the unpaired edge electrons from the graphene layer, with a significantly high value compared to the regular spin-down current. Reverse biasing of this system shows no oscillating behavior and current with lower magnitude because the electrons participating in transport in this case originated from the hBN layer. Moreover, doping the G-hBN heterostructure significantly increases the current as noted from the I-V curves and the large portion of transmission peaks entering the bias window. Spin field effect transistors from G-hBN-G that operate based on tunneling from G-layer through the hBN layer were constructed. At zero gate voltage, the device is a perfect spin-down filter (or spin-up filter after doping). A positive gate voltage leads to a significant increase in the drain current. While a negative voltage decreases it which eventually gives rise to a spin field effect transistor with an ON/OFF ratio up to 1010. Therefore, these heterostructures represent cutting-edge nanodevices for highly performant spintronics.
All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

Hazem Abdelsalam: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Nahed H. Teleb: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Wang Zhilong: Investigation, Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Yushen Liu: Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Qinfang Zhang: Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
